title: DFS traversal
description: Perform DFS traversal recursive and iterative

examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, result [0]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited), recurse to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, result [0,1], visit neighbor 0 (visited), skip, visit neighbor 3 (unvisited), recurse to node 3. Step 4: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, result [0,1,3], visit neighbor 1 (visited), skip, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited), recurse to node 2. Step 5: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, result [0,1,3,2], visit neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (visited), skip. Step 6: Backtrack to node 3, then node 1, then node 0. Final result [0,1,3,2].

Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, result [0]. Step 2: Check row 0: neighbor 1 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, result [0,1], check row 1: neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 3. Step 4: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, result [0,1,3], check row 3: neighbor 1 (visited), skip, neighbor 2 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 2. Step 5: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, result [0,1,3,2], check row 2: neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (visited), skip. Step 6: Backtrack. Final result [0,1,3,2].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive DFS
approaches.summary: Use recursion to explore each node's neighbors deeply before backtracking, leveraging the call stack for traversal. This is intuitive for tree-like structures but may cause stack overflow for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive DFS Traversal on Graph
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of recursive DFS traversal for both adjacency list and adjacency matrix graph representations.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we implement recursive DFS traversal for graphs. The intuition is that DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, making it ideal for pathfinding, cycle detection, and topological sorting. For graphs, we need to handle both adjacency list and matrix representations, track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops, and process disconnected graphs. The algorithm starts from a source node, marks it visited, processes it, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. For disconnected graphs, we iterate through all nodes and start DFS from unvisited ones. Time complexity is O(V + E) for adjacency list and O(V²) for adjacency matrix, where V is vertices and E is edges. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive DFS function as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes visited before recursion (causing infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs, and stack overflow for large graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs_recursive(graph, start, visited, result): visited.add(start); result.append(start); for neighbor in graph[start]: if neighbor not in visited: dfs_recursive(graph, neighbor, visited, result).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, result [0]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited), recurse to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, result [0,1], visit neighbor 0 (visited), skip, visit neighbor 3 (unvisited), recurse to node 3. Step 4: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, result [0,1,3], visit neighbor 1 (visited), skip, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited), recurse to node 2. Step 5: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, result [0,1,3,2], visit neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (visited), skip. Step 6: Backtrack to node 3, then node 1, then node 0. Final result [0,1,3,2].

Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, result [0]. Step 2: Check row 0: neighbor 1 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, result [0,1], check row 1: neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 3. Step 4: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, result [0,1,3], check row 3: neighbor 1 (visited), skip, neighbor 2 (value 1, unvisited), recurse to node 2. Step 5: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, result [0,1,3,2], check row 2: neighbor 0 (visited), skip, neighbor 3 (visited), skip. Step 6: Backtrack. Final result [0,1,3,2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Function for Single Component
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that performs DFS from a starting node within one connected component.
ladders.explanation: This is the key subroutine: a recursive function that performs DFS traversal starting from a given node. The intuition is that recursion naturally mimics the depth-first exploration—we go deep into one neighbor before considering others, using the call stack to remember where to backtrack. The algorithm takes a graph (adjacency list), current node, visited set, and result list. It marks the current node as visited, adds it to result, then recursively calls itself for each unvisited neighbor. Time complexity is O(V + E) for the component being traversed. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack depth. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in L0 to handle the full graph. Connection to L2: This implements the core recursive logic. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causing infinite loops in cycles) and modifying the visited set incorrectly. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, graph, visited, result): visited.add(node); result.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: dfs(neighbor, graph, visited, result).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(0). visited={0}, result=[0]. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited), recurse dfs(1). Step 2: In dfs(1), visited={0,1}, result=[0,1]. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 3 (unvisited), recurse dfs(3). Step 3: In dfs(3), visited={0,1,3}, result=[0,1,3]. Neighbors: 1 (visited, skip). Return to dfs(1). Step 4: Back in dfs(1), no more neighbors, return to dfs(0). Step 5: In dfs(0), next neighbor 2 (unvisited), recurse dfs(2). Step 6: In dfs(2), visited={0,1,3,2}, result=[0,1,3,2]. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip). Return. Final result [0,1,3,2].

Step 1: dfs(0): visited={0}, result=[0], neighbor 1 unvisited, recurse dfs(1). Step 2: dfs(1): visited={0,1}, result=[0,1], neighbor 2 unvisited, recurse dfs(2). Step 3: dfs(2): visited={0,1,2}, result=[0,1,2], neighbor 3 unvisited, recurse dfs(3). Step 4: dfs(3): visited={0,1,2,3}, result=[0,1,2,3], no neighbors. Step 5: Backtrack through dfs(2), dfs(1), dfs(0). Final result [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Exploration with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the recursive pattern of visiting a node, exploring neighbors deeply, then backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive logic: the pattern of visiting a node, recursively exploring each unvisited neighbor to completion, then backtracking to explore other paths. The intuition is that recursion provides a natural way to implement depth-first search—each recursive call represents going deeper into the graph, and returning from the call represents backtracking. The algorithm is: 1. Process current node (e.g., add to result). 2. For each neighbor: if unvisited, recursively apply the same process. 3. After exploring all neighbors, return (backtrack). Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls, typically O(V) for nodes visited. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This logic is encapsulated in the DFS function. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of graph traversal. Common mistakes include not having a base case (implicitly provided when no unvisited neighbors), and not understanding the backtracking mechanism. Real code pattern: def explore(node): process(node); for neighbor in neighbors(node): if not visited(neighbor): explore(neighbor).

examples.input: Tree structure: 0 connected to 1 and 2, 1 connected to 3, start at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Visit node 0. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1 recursively. Step 3: In visit node 1, explore neighbor 3 recursively. Step 4: In visit node 3, no neighbors, return to node 1. Step 5: In node 1, no more neighbors, return to node 0. Step 6: In node 0, explore neighbor 2 recursively. Step 7: Visit node 2, no neighbors, return to node 0. Step 8: Done. Order: 0,1,3,2.

Step 1: Visit 0. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1 recursively. Step 3: Visit 1, explore neighbor 0 (visited, skip), explore neighbor 2 recursively. Step 4: Visit 2, explore neighbor 1 (visited, skip), return to 1. Step 5: In 1, no more neighbors, return to 0. Step 6: In 0, no more neighbors (1 already visited). Order: 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse a graph while tracking visited nodes to avoid reprocessing and infinite loops.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a graph while maintaining a visited set to avoid infinite loops in cyclic graphs. The intuition is that without tracking visited nodes, we might revisit nodes endlessly in cycles, causing infinite recursion or loops. The algorithm: 1. Start with an empty visited set. 2. When visiting a node, add it to visited. 3. Only traverse to neighbors not in visited. This ensures each node is processed exactly once. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each node and edge is considered. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited set. Connection to L2: This visited tracking is essential for the recursive exploration. Connection to L4: This uses basic set operations. Common mistakes include adding to visited after recursion (causing infinite loops), using a list for visited (slow lookup), or not resetting visited between traversals. Real code pattern: visited = set(); def traverse(node): if node in visited: return; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: traverse(neighbor).

examples.input: Graph: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 0 and 2, start at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, visited={}. Add 0 to visited: {0}. Step 2: Traverse to neighbor 1 (not in visited). Step 3: At 1, add to visited: {0,1}. Traverse to neighbor 0 (in visited, skip). Traverse to neighbor 2 (not in visited). Step 4: At 2, add to visited: {0,1,2}. No neighbors. Step 5: Backtrack. Final visited {0,1,2}.

examples.input: Graph: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 2, 2 connected to 0, start at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, visited={}. Add 0: {0}. Step 2: Traverse to neighbor 1 (not visited). Step 3: At 1, add 1: {0,1}. Traverse to neighbor 2 (not visited). Step 4: At 2, add 2: {0,1,2}. Traverse to neighbor 0 (visited, skip). Step 5: Backtrack. Final visited {0,1,2}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Graph Neighbors
ladders.desc: Learn to retrieve and iterate through neighbors of a node in graph representations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing the neighbors of a node in a graph, which is crucial for traversal. Graphs can be represented as adjacency lists (dictionary of lists) or adjacency matrices (2D list). For adjacency list: graph[node] returns a list of neighbors. For adjacency matrix: iterate through row[node] to find indices where value is 1 (or non-zero). The intuition is that traversal requires knowing which nodes are connected to the current node. Time complexity: O(degree(node)) for adjacency list, O(V) for adjacency matrix. Space complexity: O(1) for access. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the traversal loop. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding graph representations. Common mistakes include assuming symmetric edges in directed graphs, off-by-one errors in matrix indices, or not handling missing nodes in adjacency lists. Real code pattern: For adjacency list: neighbors = graph[node]; for neighbor in neighbors: ... For adjacency matrix: for i in range(len(matrix[node])): if matrix[node][i] == 1: neighbor = i.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] which returns [1, 2]. Step 2: These are the nodes directly connected to node 0. Step 3: To iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2.

Step 1: Row for node 1 is [1,0,1] (indices 0,1,2). Step 2: Check each index: index 0 has value 1, so neighbor 0; index 1 has value 0, skip; index 2 has value 1, so neighbor 2. Step 3: Neighbors are [0, 2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation Basics
ladders.desc: Understand what a graph is and how to represent it as adjacency list or matrix.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding graphs and their representations. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between nodes). For DFS, we need a way to represent which nodes are connected. Two common representations are: 1. Adjacency List: A dictionary where each key is a node, and the value is a list of its neighboring nodes. Efficient for sparse graphs. Example: {0: [1, 2], 1: [0], 2: [0]} means node 0 connects to 1 and 2, node 1 connects to 0, node 2 connects to 0. 2. Adjacency Matrix: A 2D list (matrix) where matrix[i][j] = 1 if there is an edge from node i to j, else 0. Efficient for dense graphs. Example: [[0,1,1],[1,0,0],[1,0,0]] represents the same graph. The intuition is that these data structures allow us to query connections quickly. Time to access neighbors: O(degree) for list, O(V) for matrix. Space: O(V + E) for list, O(V²) for matrix. Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to access neighbors. Common mistakes include confusing directed vs undirected graphs (symmetric in undirected), zero-based vs one-based indexing, and not initializing the data structure correctly. Real code pattern: Adjacency list: graph = {0: [1, 2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}. Adjacency matrix: graph = [[0,1,1],[1,0,0],[1,0,0]].

examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge 0-1: in list, add 1 to neighbors of 0, add 0 to neighbors of 1. Step 3: Edge 0-2: add 2 to neighbors of 0, add 0 to neighbors of 2. Step 4: List: {0:[1,2], 1:[0], 2:[0]}. Step 5: For matrix: create 3x3 zeros. Set matrix[0][1]=1, matrix[1][0]=1 for edge 0-1. Set matrix[0][2]=1, matrix[2][0]=1 for edge 0-2. Result: [[0,1,1],[1,0,0],[1,0,0]].

Step 1: Nodes 0 and 1. Step 2: Edge 0-1: in list, neighbors of 0: [1], neighbors of 1: [0]. Step 3: List: {0:[1], 1:[0]}. Step 4: Matrix 2x2: set matrix[0][1]=1, matrix[1][0]=1. Others 0. Result: [[0,1],[1,0]].

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative DFS with Stack
approaches.summary: Simulate recursion using an explicit stack to push and pop nodes, exploring neighbors in a last-in-first-out order. This avoids recursion limits and provides more control over traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Iterative DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement iterative DFS traversal for a graph using an explicit stack, handling both adjacency list representation and visited tracking.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for performing DFS traversal iteratively using a stack. The intuition is to simulate the recursive call stack manually, which gives us more control over the traversal process and avoids recursion depth limits. We start by pushing the starting node onto the stack and marking it as visited. Then, while the stack isn't empty, we pop a node, process it (add to result), and push all its unvisited neighbors onto the stack. This LIFO (last-in-first-out) order ensures we explore depth-first: we go as deep as possible along one branch before backtracking. The algorithm works because the stack maintains the frontier of nodes to explore, with the most recently discovered nodes explored first, mimicking recursion's behavior. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty result list, visited set, and stack with start node. 2. Mark start as visited. 3. While stack not empty: pop node, append to result, for each neighbor not visited: push to stack and mark visited. 4. Return result. Time complexity is O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack and visited set. This connects to the full problem by implementing the complete traversal method. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes as visited when pushing them (leading to duplicates) or using the wrong order for neighbor traversal (affecting output order but still valid DFS). Real code pattern: def dfs_iterative(graph, start): result = []; visited = set(); stack = [start]; visited.add(start); while stack: node = stack.pop(); result.append(node); for neighbor in reversed(graph[node]): if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor); visited.add(neighbor); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize: result = [], visited = {0}, stack = [0]. Step 2: Pop 0, result = [0], neighbors [1,2]. Push 2 (unvisited), visited = {0,2}, stack = [2]. Push 1 (unvisited), visited = {0,2,1}, stack = [2,1]. Step 3: Pop 1, result = [0,1], neighbors [0,3]. 0 visited, skip. Push 3 (unvisited), visited = {0,2,1,3}, stack = [2,3]. Step 4: Pop 3, result = [0,1,3], neighbors [1]. 1 visited, skip. stack = [2]. Step 5: Pop 2, result = [0,1,3,2], neighbors [0,4]. 0 visited, skip. Push 4 (unvisited), visited = {0,2,1,3,4}, stack = [4]. Step 6: Pop 4, result = [0,1,3,2,4], neighbors [2]. 2 visited, skip. stack empty. Return [0,1,3,2,4] (Note: order may vary based on neighbor processing; this trace uses reversed order for consistency).

Step 1: result = [], visited = {1}, stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, result = [1], neighbors [2,3]. Push 3, visited = {1,3}, stack = [3]. Push 2, visited = {1,3,2}, stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, result = [1,2], neighbors [1,4]. 1 visited, skip. Push 4, visited = {1,3,2,4}, stack = [3,4]. Step 4: Pop 4, result = [1,2,4], neighbors [2]. 2 visited, skip. stack = [3]. Step 5: Pop 3, result = [1,2,4,3], neighbors [1]. 1 visited, skip. stack empty. Return [1,2,4,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Management for Node Exploration
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that pops nodes from a stack, processes them, and pushes unvisited neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of iterative DFS, where we manage the stack to explore nodes in depth-first order. The intuition is that by using a stack (LIFO structure), we always explore the most recently discovered node first, which mimics the backtracking behavior of recursion. This ensures we go deep along a path before exploring other branches. The algorithm: 1. Start with a stack containing the initial node and a visited set with that node. 2. While the stack is not empty: pop the top node, process it (e.g., add to result), then for each neighbor of that node, if not visited, push it onto the stack and mark as visited. This loop continues until all reachable nodes are processed. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(V + E) in the context of the full graph, as it processes each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack and visited set. This feeds into L0 by providing the main traversal logic; L0 adds graph input handling and output formatting. Common mistakes include pushing neighbors without marking them visited immediately (causing duplicates) or processing nodes in the wrong order (e.g., using a queue instead of stack for BFS). Real code pattern: while stack: node = stack.pop(); process(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor); visited.add(neighbor).

examples.input: graph: {A: [B, C], B: [A], C: [A]}, start: A, visited initially {A}, stack initially [A]
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = [A], visited = {A}. Pop A, process A. Neighbors B, C: push C (unvisited), visited = {A,C}, stack = [C]; push B (unvisited), visited = {A,C,B}, stack = [C,B]. Step 2: Pop B, process B. Neighbors A: A visited, skip. stack = [C]. Step 3: Pop C, process C. Neighbors A: A visited, skip. stack empty. Processed order: A, B, C (if neighbors processed in original order) or A, C, B (if reversed).

examples.input: graph: {1: [2], 2: [3], 3: []}, start: 1, visited initially {1}, stack initially [1]
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = [1], visited = {1}. Pop 1, process 1. Neighbors 2: push 2, visited = {1,2}, stack = [2]. Step 2: Pop 2, process 2. Neighbors 3: push 3, visited = {1,2,3}, stack = [3]. Step 3: Pop 3, process 3. Neighbors none. stack empty. Processed: 1,2,3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — LIFO Stack for Depth-First Exploration
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the use of a stack to ensure depth-first traversal order.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight: using a stack (Last-In-First-Out data structure) to achieve depth-first exploration. The intuition is that in DFS, we want to explore as deep as possible along a branch before backtracking. A stack naturally supports this because when we push nodes onto it, the last node pushed (the most recently discovered) is the first one popped and explored next. This mimics the call stack in recursive DFS, where the most recent recursive call is completed first. The algorithm involves: 1. Pushing the starting node onto an empty stack. 2. Repeatedly popping a node, processing it, and pushing its unvisited neighbors. This ensures we always go deeper from the current node before exploring other nodes at the same level. For example, if we have node A with neighbors B and C, pushing B then C means C is popped first, so we explore C's subtree before B's. Time complexity for this logic is O(V) for node operations, but in full context, it contributes to O(V + E). Space complexity is O(V) for the stack. This connects to L1 by providing the fundamental mechanism that the sub-routine uses; L1 adds neighbor processing and visited tracking. Common mistakes include using a queue (FIFO) instead, which results in BFS, or not reversing neighbor order if a specific output order is needed. Real code pattern: stack = [start]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); for neighbor in neighbors: stack.append(neighbor).

examples.input: Start: A, neighbors function: A->[B,C], B->[D], C->[E], D->[], E->[]
examples.output: Traversal order: A, C, E, B, D
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = [A]. Pop A, push B, C -> stack = [B, C]. Step 2: Pop C, push E -> stack = [B, E]. Step 3: Pop E, no push -> stack = [B]. Step 4: Pop B, push D -> stack = [D]. Step 5: Pop D, no push -> stack empty. Order: A, C, E, B, D.

Step 1: Stack = [1]. Pop 1, push 2,3 -> stack = [2,3]. Step 2: Pop 3, push 5 -> stack = [2,5]. Step 3: Pop 5, no push -> stack = [2]. Step 4: Pop 2, push 4 -> stack = [4]. Step 5: Pop 4, no push -> stack empty. Order: 1,3,5,2,4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a graph using an adjacency list and access neighbors for traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers how to represent a graph as an adjacency list, which is essential for DFS traversal. An adjacency list is a dictionary or list where each key is a node, and the value is a list of its neighboring nodes. The intuition is that it provides efficient access to neighbors, which is crucial for exploring the graph during DFS. For example, in a graph with nodes 0,1,2 and edges 0-1 and 0-2, the adjacency list is {0: [1,2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V + E)) and allows quick neighbor lookup. The algorithm involves: 1. Defining the graph structure, e.g., using a Python dictionary. 2. Given a node, retrieving its list of neighbors to push onto the stack during DFS. This connects to L2 by providing the data structure that the stack logic operates on; L2 uses this to get neighbors for pushing. Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(1) on average, and space complexity is O(V + E) for storing the graph. Common mistakes include using an adjacency matrix (less efficient for sparse graphs) or incorrectly defining bidirectional edges (for undirected graphs, each edge must appear in both nodes' lists). Real code pattern: graph = {0: [1,2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}; neighbors_of_0 = graph[0]  # returns [1,2].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty dict. Step 2: For edge (0,1): add 1 to list for key 0, add 0 to list for key 1 -> {0: [1], 1: [0]}. Step 3: For edge (0,2): add 2 to list for key 0, add 0 to list for key 2 -> {0: [1,2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}. Step 4: For edge (1,2): add 2 to list for key 1, add 1 to list for key 2 -> {0: [1,2], 1: [0,2], 2: [0,1]}.

Step 1: Initialize dict. Step 2: Edge (A,B): add B to A's list, add A to B's list -> {'A': ['B'], 'B': ['A']}. Step 3: Edge (B,C): add C to B's list, add B to C's list -> {'A': ['B'], 'B': ['A','C'], 'C': ['B']}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Push and Pop on a Stack
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental stack operations: push (append) and pop.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of a stack data structure: push (adding an element to the top) and pop (removing and returning the top element). The intuition is that a stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) order, meaning the last item added is the first one removed. This is crucial for DFS because it ensures we explore the most recently discovered node first, leading to depth-first traversal. In Python, a list can be used as a stack with append() for push and pop() for pop. For example, stack = []; stack.append(1) pushes 1; stack.append(2) pushes 2; top = stack.pop() returns 2. The algorithm involves: 1. Initializing an empty list. 2. Using append() to add elements. 3. Using pop() to remove elements, which by default removes the last item. Time complexity for push and pop is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to L3 by providing the operations used to manage nodes in the adjacency list during traversal; L3 uses these to build the graph structure. Common mistakes include using pop(0) (which removes from the front, making it a queue) or not handling empty stack pops (which causes errors). Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(5); item = stack.pop().

examples.input: Operations: push A, push B, pop, push C, pop, pop
examples.output: Result: pop returns B, then C, then A; final stack empty
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push A -> stack = [A]. Step 3: push B -> stack = [A, B]. Step 4: pop -> returns B, stack = [A]. Step 5: push C -> stack = [A, C]. Step 6: pop -> returns C, stack = [A]. Step 7: pop -> returns A, stack = [].

examples.input: Operations: push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop, push 4, pop
examples.output: Result: pop returns 3, then 2, then 4; stack has [1]
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push 1 -> [1]. Step 3: push 2 -> [1,2]. Step 4: push 3 -> [1,2,3]. Step 5: pop -> returns 3, stack = [1,2]. Step 6: pop -> returns 2, stack = [1]. Step 7: push 4 -> [1,4]. Step 8: pop -> returns 4, stack = [1].

testCases.input: Operations: push A, push B, push C, pop, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns C, then B, stack has [A]

testCases.input: Operations: push 5, push 6, pop, push 7, pop
Operations: push X, pop, push Y, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns X, then Y, stack empty

testCases.input: Operations: push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns 3, then 2, then 1, stack empty

testCases.input: Operations: push 10, push 20, pop, push 30, pop, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns 20, then 30, then 10, stack empty

testCases.input: Operations: push a, push b, push c, push d, pop, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns d, then c, stack has [a, b]

testCases.input: Operations: push 0, push 1, pop, push 2, push 3, pop, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns 1, then 3, then 2, stack has [0]

testCases.input: Operations: push start, push mid, push end, pop, pop, push last, pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns end, then mid, then last, stack has [start]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Graph?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic concept of a graph as a collection of nodes and edges.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a graph is a data structure consisting of nodes (also called vertices) and edges that connect pairs of nodes. The intuition is that graphs model relationships or connections between entities, such as social networks (people as nodes, friendships as edges) or road maps (cities as nodes, roads as edges). Nodes represent discrete points, and edges represent the links between them. Graphs can be directed (edges have direction, like one-way streets) or undirected (edges are bidirectional, like friendships). For DFS traversal, we typically use undirected graphs or treat directed graphs accordingly. The basic idea is to start at one node and explore connected nodes systematically. This connects to L4 by providing the context for why we use stacks; L4's stack operations are applied to navigate graphs. Time and space complexity aren't directly applicable here, but understanding graphs is prerequisite for analyzing traversal algorithms. Common mistakes include confusing graphs with trees (trees are acyclic connected graphs) or not recognizing that nodes can be any hashable data type. Real code pattern: In abstract terms, a graph can be thought of as a set of nodes and a set of edges, e.g., nodes = {0,1,2}, edges = {(0,1), (1,2)}.

examples.input: Nodes: A, B, C; Edges: A-B, B-C
examples.output: Graph: A connected to B, B connected to C, A not directly connected to C
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: A, B, C. Step 2: Edge A-B means A and B are connected. Step 3: Edge B-C means B and C are connected. Step 4: Overall, A can reach C via B, but there's no direct edge A-C. This forms a linear graph: A -- B -- C.

examples.output: Graph: Triangle where each node is connected to the other two
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 1,2,3. Step 2: Edge 1-2 connects 1 and 2. Step 3: Edge 2-3 connects 2 and 3. Step 4: Edge 3-1 connects 3 and 1. Step 5: This creates a cycle: 1 is connected to 2 and 3, 2 to 1 and 3, 3 to 1 and 2.

testCases.expected: Graph: A connected to B and C, B and C not connected
Graph: Single node P with no edges

testCases.input: Nodes: A,B,C,D; Edges: A-B, B-C, C-D, D-A
Nodes: X,Y,Z; Edges: X-Y, Y-Z, Z-X

testCases.expected: Graph: Single edge between 1 and 2 (undirected, so same as one edge)
